perturbation.original_text: CONSULTANT shall be compensated and reimbursed for the services as set forth in the attached Statement of Work. Completeness of work product shall be determined by REIT in its sole discretion, and CONSULTANT agrees to make all revisions, additions, deletions or alterations as requested by the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: CONSULTANT shall be compensated and reimbursed for services as generally described in the attached Statement of Work. Determination of the work product's suitability will be at the discretion of the REIT, and the Consultant will endeavor to address revisions as reasonably possible.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states that the consultant 'shall be compensated and reimbursed for the services as set forth in the attached Statement of Work.' Changing this to 'services as generally described' introduces ambiguity, as it's unclear what level of deviation from the SOW is acceptable. Similarly, changing 'Completeness of work product shall be determined by REIT in its sole discretion, and CONSULTANT agrees to make all revisions' to 'Determination of the work product's suitability will be at the discretion of the REIT, and the Consultant will endeavor to address revisions as reasonably possible' changes a mandatory requirement to a discretionary one, weakening the obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: Virginia Code § 8.2-204 addresses the formation of contracts. By making the consultant's obligation to perform revisions 'reasonably possible' and by generally describing services (instead of explicitly), it creates a very vague obligation on the Consultant, it could be argued the changes create uncertainty regarding essential terms (scope of services and required revisions). This ambiguity could lead to a dispute about whether the contract is enforceable and what the actual obligations of the consultant are, especially if the consultant does not complete the revisions. The original contract clearly established the consultant was required to make all changes - thus fully compliant work was mandated.

Commercial Code - Sales » Part 2. Form, Formation and Readjustment of Contract » § 8.2-204. Formation in general Creating a Report: Check the sections you'd like to appear in the report, then use the "Create Report" button at the bottom of the page to generate your report. Once the report is generated you'll then have the option to download it as a pdf, print or email the report. Code of Virginia Title 8.2. Commercial Code - Sales Part 2. Form, Formation and Readjustment of Contract 6/15/2025 § 8.2-204 . Formation in general. (1) A contract for sale of goods may be made in any manner sufficient to show agreement, including conduct by both parties which recognizes the existence of such a contract. (2) An agreement sufficient to constitute a contract for sale may be found even though the moment of its making is undetermined. (3) Even though one or more terms are left open a contract for sale does not fail for indefiniteness if the parties have intended to make a contract and there is a reasonably certain basis for giving an appropriate remedy. 1964, c. 219. The chapters of the acts of assembly referenced in the historical citation at the end of this section may not constitute a comprehensive list of such chapters and may exclude chapters whose provisions have expired. perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the consultant's obligations, weakening the enforceability of the contract. The original text mandates specific actions ('shall be compensated and reimbursed for the services as set forth in the attached Statement of Work' and 'CONSULTANT agrees to make all revisions'), while the changed text uses vaguer language ('services as generally described' and 'Consultant will endeavor to address revisions as reasonably possible'). Although the scraped law snippets do not directly address this issue, the LLM explanation uses Virginia Code § 8.2-204 to justify why this change creates uncertainty about essential terms. Since there is a weakening of the legal requirement to have fully compliant work, which now only requires 'reasonably possible' revisions, the modification introduces ambiguity which makes the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: CONSULTANT agrees that at all times during or subsequent to the performance of the consulting services under this Agreement, CONSULTANT will keep confidential and will not divulge, communicate, or use Proprietary Information, except for CONSULTANT's own use during the Term of this Agreement to the extent necessary to perform the consulting services.
perturbation.changed_text: CONSULTANT agrees that at all times during or subsequent to the performance of the consulting services under this Agreement, CONSULTANT will endeavor to keep confidential and not unnecessarily divulge Proprietary Information, except to the degree reasonably required for CONSULTANT's activities during the Term of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text imposes a strict duty on the consultant to keep proprietary information confidential. The modified text weakens this by stating the consultant will 'endeavor' to keep confidential and not 'unnecessarily' divulge, and using 'to the degree reasonably required' instead of 'to the extent necessary.' 'Endeavor', 'unnecessarily,' and 'reasonably required' are all vague terms that allow for subjective interpretation.
perturbation.law_explanation: VUTSA aims to protect trade secrets by preventing their misappropriation. By weakening the confidentiality obligation, the modified text makes it more difficult to prove misappropriation. The original language created a strict duty of confidentiality, while the modified version allows the consultant to potentially disclose proprietary information under the guise of acting 'reasonably' or arguing that disclosure was not 'unnecessary,' thereby undermining the protection afforded by the VUTSA. The original, without the ambiguities, provided greater and clearer protection of trade secrets.

Once the report is generated you'll then have the option to download it as a pdf, print or email the report. Code of Virginia Title 59.1. Trade and Commerce Chapter 26. Uniform Trade Secrets Act 6/15/2025 § 59.1-336 . Short title and definitions. As used in this chapter, which may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, unless the context requires otherwise: "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, use of a computer or computer network without authority, breach of a duty or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. "Misappropriation" means: 1. Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or 2. Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who a. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or b. At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was (1) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (2) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; (3) Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (4) Acquired by accident or mistake. "Person" means a natural pe
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguities that weaken the original confidentiality obligation, potentially conflicting with the intent of VUTSA to protect trade secrets. The original contract imposed a strict duty, whereas the modified text uses terms like 'endeavor,' 'unnecessarily,' and 'reasonably required,' which allow for subjective interpretation and could make it more difficult to prove misappropriation. Snippet 1 defines 'improper means' which includes 'breach of a duty to maintain secrecy'. By weakening this duty, the modified text provides an avenue for the consultant to argue that their actions, even if disclosing proprietary information, were not a breach, undermining the protection VUTSA intends to provide.

perturbation.original_text: CONSULTANT agrees that during its consultancy for REIT and for a period of twelve (12) months immediately following the termination of its consultancy with the Company for any reason, whether with or without cause, it will not: (a) solicit, entice or induce any Customer for the purpose of providing, or provide, products or services that are competitive with the products or services provided by the Company, or (b) solicit, entice, or induce any Customer to terminate or reduce its business with (or refrain from increasing its business with) the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: CONSULTANT agrees to use its best judgement and ethical standards during its consultancy for REIT and for a period of twelve (12) months immediately following the termination of its consultancy with the Company for any reason, whether with or without cause, in determining whether to solicit, entice or induce any Customer for the purpose of providing, or provide, products or services that are competitive with the products or services provided by the Company, or solicit, entice, or induce any Customer to terminate or reduce its business with (or refrain from increasing its business with) the Company.
perturbation.explanation: The original text has a clearly defined action the consultant must NOT do. Introducing 'best judgement and ethical standards in determining whether' creates ambiguity that a court would need to interperet. What is defined as 'best judgement and ethical standards'?
perturbation.law_citation: § 59.1-9.5. Agreements in restraint of trade or commerce unlawful; exceptions
perturbation.law_explanation: While the original non-solicitation agreement is relatively clear and enforceable (subject to reasonableness), the modified language makes it more difficult to prove a violation. The ambiguity introduced by "best judgment and ethical standards" allows the consultant to argue that their actions, even if they resulted in solicitation of customers, were taken in good faith based on their subjective judgment. This makes it harder to enforce the non-solicitation agreement and pursue antitrust claims if the consultant's actions actually restrained trade. The consultant could argue that, based on their 'best judgment', their actions were not anti-competitive, even if they violated the spirit of the original agreement. The goal of the Antitrust Act is to prevent behaviors that could affect normal competition - this makes the violation more ambiguous than the original clause, which was very clear about that sort of behaviour.

Virginia Antitrust Act 6/15/2025 § 59.1-9.5 . Contracts, etc., in restraint of trade unlawful. Every contract, combination or conspiracy in restraint of trade or commerce of this Commonwealth is unlawful. 1974, c. 545. The chapters of the acts of assembly referenced in the historical citation at the end of this section may not constitute a comprehensive list of such chapters and may exclude chapters whose provisions have expired. perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity by adding 'best judgement and ethical standards' when deciding to solicit customers. This contradicts the original contract's clear prohibition, potentially undermining the intent to prevent unfair competition, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that 'Every contract, combination or conspiracy in restraint of trade or commerce of this Commonwealth is unlawful.' While the original clause was clear in preventing specific behaviour, the modified text introduces ambiguity, making it more difficult to prove a violation of this law because the consultant could argue their actions were based on 'best judgement,' even if they restrained trade.
